article.title: Harvard Researchers Find That a Dash of Randomness Keeps Robot Swarms From Gridlocking
article.summary: A PNAS study from Harvard SEAS shows crowded robot swarms move most efficiently when agents inject a tuned amount of noise into their paths, breaking symmetry-driven jams without descending into aimless wandering.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

A team of applied mathematicians and roboticists at Harvard has shown that crowded swarms of simple agents move most efficiently not when each machine follows the shortest path to its goal, but when every agent injects a measured amount of randomness into its trajectory. The finding, published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, suggests that a carefully tuned dose of algorithmic noise is a cheaper and more robust way to prevent gridlock than complex central coordination, [according to the Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences](https://seas.harvard.edu/news/too-many-cooks-or-too-many-robots).

## What We Know

The study, titled "Noise-enabled goal attainment in crowded collectives," was led by applied mathematics Ph.D. student Lucy Liu and supervised by L. Mahadevan, the Lola England de Valpine Professor of Applied Mathematics, Organismic and Evolutionary Biology, and Physics at Harvard, [as reported by ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260414075639.htm). The co-authors include SEAS Senior Research Fellow Justin Werfel and Eindhoven University of Technology physicist Federico Toschi.

The researchers combined large-scale computer simulations with physical experiments using small wheeled robots tracked via overhead cameras and QR codes at Eindhoven, [according to ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260414075639.htm). When simulated agents were allowed to "beeline" directly toward their goals, dense traffic jams formed almost immediately and the entire swarm seized up; when their movements were made too random, the jams disappeared but the agents wasted so much time wandering that overall throughput collapsed, [the Harvard SEAS announcement notes](https://seas.harvard.edu/news/too-many-cooks-or-too-many-robots).

Between those extremes the team identified a "Goldilocks zone" in which agents occasionally take a random sideways or backward step. That localized, seemingly wasteful detour breaks the symmetry of an incipient jam, opens a momentary spatial vacuum, and lets the rest of the swarm keep flowing, [the Harvard release explains](https://seas.harvard.edu/news/too-many-cooks-or-too-many-robots). The team distilled the observation into formulas linking crowd density and noise level to a "goal attainment rate," allowing the optimal amount of randomness to be computed for a given configuration, [according to ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260414075639.htm).

"This might be counterintuitive, because how could randomness make things easier to work with?" Liu said in the Harvard announcement, [as quoted by Tech Xplore](https://techxplore.com/news/2026-04-cooks-robots-goldilocks-randomness-robot.html). The same write-up lists oil spill cleanup, machine assembly, coordinated robotic fleets, and crowded public spaces as potential application domains, [per Tech Xplore](https://techxplore.com/news/2026-04-cooks-robots-goldilocks-randomness-robot.html).

The full paper is indexed under DOI 10.1073/pnas.2519032123 in [the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2519032123). Funding came from National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship DGE 2140743, the Simons Foundation, and the Henri Seydoux Fund, [according to ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260414075639.htm).

## What We Don't Know

Neither the Harvard announcement nor the secondary coverage publishes a headline figure for how much efficiency improves at the optimal noise level, or how sharply the sweet spot falls off at higher crowd densities, [according to the Harvard SEAS release](https://seas.harvard.edu/news/too-many-cooks-or-too-many-robots). It is also unclear how well the result generalizes beyond simple wheeled agents with uniform goals to heterogeneous fleets, asymmetric tasks, or environments with moving obstacles and humans in the loop; the published experiments used identical robots on an open testbed, [as described by ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260414075639.htm).

The paper does not claim that noise-based navigation is superior to every centrally coordinated routing scheme used in real warehouses today, only that it offers an effective decentralized alternative that scales without a global planner, [according to the Harvard release](https://seas.harvard.edu/news/too-many-cooks-or-too-many-robots).

## Analysis

The Harvard result sits in a long tradition, from flocking models to ant-colony routing, of showing that simple local rules and a touch of stochasticity can outperform rigid optimization in crowded collectives. Its practical interest for robotics lies in being cheap: adding a tuned random term to an existing navigation stack is far less expensive than running a centralized traffic controller over every warehouse aisle or delivery corridor. Whether operators of commercial fleets adopt it will depend on how the Goldilocks zone holds up once the agents are no longer identical, the floor plan is no longer symmetric, and the cost of a "suboptimal sideways step" is measured in real dollars per mispicked order.
